title: Loir-et-Cher

infobox.key: • [President of the Departmental Council](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_departmental_councils_(France) "List of presidents of departmental councils (France)")
infobox.value: Philippe Gouet[[1]](#cite_note-1) ([UDI](/wiki/Union_of_Democrats_and_Independents "Union of Democrats and Independents"))
French Land Register data, which exclude [estuaries](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary"), and lakes, ponds, and glaciers larger than 1 km2 | |

([/ˌlwɑːr eɪ ˈʃɛər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); French: [[lwaʁ e ʃɛʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-WikiLucas00-Loir-et-Cher.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-WikiLucas00-Loir-et-Cher.wav")) is a [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") in the [Centre-Val de Loire](/wiki/Centre-Val_de_Loire "Centre-Val de Loire") [region](/wiki/Regions_of_France "Regions of France") of [France](/wiki/France "France"). It is named after two rivers which run through it, the [Loir](/wiki/Loir "Loir") in its northern part and the [Cher](/wiki/Cher_(river) "Cher (river)") in its southern part. Its prefecture is [Blois](/wiki/Blois "Blois"). The [INSEE](/wiki/Institut_national_de_la_statistique_et_des_%C3%A9tudes_%C3%A9conomiques "Institut national de la statistique et des études économiques") and La Poste gave it the number 41. It had a population of 328,543 in 2023.[[3]](#cite_note-pophist-3)

outgoing_links.target_title: Institut national de la statistique et des études économiques

**Loir-et-Cher** ([/ˌlwɑːr eɪ ˈʃɛər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); French: [[lwaʁ e ʃɛʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-WikiLucas00-Loir-et-Cher.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-WikiLucas00-Loir-et-Cher.wav")) is a [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") in the [Centre-Val de Loire](/wiki/Centre-Val_de_Loire "Centre-Val de Loire") [region](/wiki/Regions_of_France "Regions of France") of [France](/wiki/France "France"). It is named after two rivers which run through it, the [Loir](/wiki/Loir "Loir") in its northern part and the [Cher](/wiki/Cher_(river) "Cher (river)") in its southern part. Its prefecture is [Blois](/wiki/Blois "Blois"). The [INSEE](/wiki/Institut_national_de_la_statistique_et_des_%C3%A9tudes_%C3%A9conomiques "Institut national de la statistique et des études économiques") and La Poste gave it the number 41. It had a population of 328,543 in 2023.[[3]](#cite_note-pophist-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loir-et-Cher&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The department of Loir-et-Cher covers a territory which had a substantial population during the prehistoric period. However it was not until the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") that local inhabitants built various castles and other fortifications to enable them to withstand a series of invasions of [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans"), [Burgundians](/wiki/Burgundians "Burgundians"), the [English](/wiki/English_people "English people") and others.

The economy is quite flourishing: there are shops in the valley, and agriculture is prominent in the region of the Beauce and the Perche to the Sologne which were prosperous until the 17th century. However, politically, the region remained quartered between the neighboring earldoms and duchies. In 1397, the House of Orleans became the possession of the Comté of Blois. In 1497, Louis d’Orleans (23rd hereditary Count of Blois) was crowned [Louis XII](/wiki/Louis_XII "Louis XII"). This marked the beginning of the importance of Blois and of the Blaisois in the political life of the French, especially under the last Valois. During that period, kings and financiers competed to build castles and elegant abodes which now form an important part of the French national heritage. (Chambord, Blois, Cheverny and so on.)

This was followed by a period of extremely violent religious wars during [Charles IX](/wiki/Charles_IX_of_France "Charles IX of France")'s reign.
In 1576 and 1588, the General Estates convened in Blois.
L’Orléanais, le Berry, la Touraine, le Perche et le Maine occupied le Loir-et-Cher and its provinces in 1790.
The Loir-et-Cher's birth as a department was very difficult and laborious.
On 29 September 1789, the constitution's advisory board made a report in which it wanted to attribute one of the 80 departments to Blois. However, some cities and canton capitals, such as Tours and Orleans, disagreed. Within the department, Montrichard turned to Amboise and Tours, Saint-Aignan wanted to turn to the Berry, and Salbris to Vierzon.

Finally, Orleans gave Blois an important part of the Sologne except Beaugency and Tours didn't give Amboise.
The department was founded 4 March 1790, in accordance with the law of 22 December 1789. It is constituted of some old provinces of the Orleanais and of the Touraine along with a part of the Berry (the left bank of the Selles en Berry's Cher which became Selles sur Cher, to Saint-Aignan).
It is mostly due to these tribulations that the department is narrow in the centre, yet reaches its widest extent beyond the Loir on the North and the Cher on the South.
After the coalition victory at the battle of Waterloo (18 June 1815), the Prussian troops occupied the department from June 1815 to November 1818.

The poet [Pierre de Ronsard](/wiki/Pierre_de_Ronsard "Pierre de Ronsard"), the inventor [Denis Papin](/wiki/Denis_Papin "Denis Papin"), and the historian [Augustin Thierry](/wiki/Augustin_Thierry "Augustin Thierry") come from here. Other well-known people are also associated with this department, such as [François the First](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France"), [Gaston d’Orleans](/wiki/Gaston,_Duke_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "Gaston, Duke of Orléans"), the Marshall [Maunoury](/wiki/Auguste-Fran%C3%A7ois_Maunoury "Auguste-François Maunoury"), and the abbot Gregoire (Bishop of Blois, elected at the Constituante). In the artistic domain, there is the compositor [Antoine Boesset](/wiki/Antoine_Bo%C3%ABsset "Antoine Boësset") (1587–1643), musician in the Louis XII[[4]](#cite_note-4) de France's court, who was the head of the Music of the King's Bedroom from 1623 to 1643.

The Loir-et-Cher's department is a part of the Centre-Val de Loire Region. It is adjacent of these departments : the Eure-et-Loir, the Loiret, the Cher, the Indre, the Indre-et-Loire and the Sarthe.
Due to its surface area of 6 343 km2, it is the 31st largest department in the nation. It has a privileged geographical situation because it is in the center of the Centre region and near the Paris basin.
An axe lively and dynamic, brings Blois closer (the department's administrative center) to both the urban conglomerations near it: Orleans and Tours.
Located on the boundaries of the Perche, the Beauce, the Sologne and the Touraine, it finds its territorial identity in the diversity of its geography and its landscapes. Cut in its middle by the Loire, it shows an image of balance and diversity.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loir-et-Cher&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Loir-et-Cher is a part of the modern region of [Centre-Val de Loire](/wiki/Centre-Val_de_Loire "Centre-Val de Loire"). Adjacent departments are [Eure-et-Loir](/wiki/Eure-et-Loir "Eure-et-Loir") to the north, [Loiret](/wiki/Loiret "Loiret") to the north-east, [Cher](/wiki/Cher_(department) "Cher (department)") to the south-east, [Indre](/wiki/Indre "Indre") to the south, [Indre-et-Loire](/wiki/Indre-et-Loire "Indre-et-Loire") to the south-west, and [Sarthe](/wiki/Sarthe "Sarthe") to the west.

The department comprises 6,314 km2, which makes it the 31st largest of the French departments in terms of area. The line of the river [Loire](/wiki/Loire_(river) "Loire (river)") traverses the land, ensuring easy communication between its own capital, [Blois](/wiki/Blois "Blois"), and the vibrant cultural and commercial centres of [Tours](/wiki/Tours "Tours") to the west and the fringes of the [Seine-Paris basin](/wiki/Seine "Seine") at [Orléans](/wiki/Orl%C3%A9ans "Orléans") to the east.

Its main rivers are the [Loire](/wiki/Loire_(river) "Loire (river)"), on which its [prefecture](/wiki/Prefectures_in_France "Prefectures in France") (capital) [Blois](/wiki/Blois "Blois") is situated, the [Loir](/wiki/Loir "Loir") and the [Cher](/wiki/Cher_(river) "Cher (river)").

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loir-et-Cher&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Politics")]

### Filming location – Heart of Oak

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loir-et-Cher&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Filming location – Heart of Oak")]

[Heart of Oak (Le Chêne)](/wiki/Heart_of_Oak_(film) "Heart of Oak (film)"), a 2022 French nature documentary directed by Laurent Charbonnier and Michel Seydoux, was filmed around a 210-year-old oak tree located in the Sologne region of Loir-et-Cher. The tree stands approximately 900 m from the home of co-director Laurent Charbonnier, offering filmmakers intimate access to its changing seasonal ecosystem.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
